data.title: Robinhood: The Financial Super-App Is Flexing Its Success
data.description:
Poll a majority of investors, and chances are that most of us wouldn't have predicted such a strong turnout for the stock market this year, especially around tariff-related uncertainties and macroeconomic carnage. Investors have resumed their "risk on" attitude and piled into volatile assets, and few companies are benefiting as much as Robinhood (NASDAQ:HOOD).

Robinhood, the digital brokerage that is now aiming to become the modern-day financial super-app, has enjoyed a sharp surge in trading volatility, particularly in the asset classes that generate the most revenue for the company: options and crypto. Year to date, shares of Robinhood have surged nearly 3x, and though I'm not necessarily saying the stock can 3x again or even double from here, I do think there's still steam left to go in this rally.

I last wrote a buy opinion on Robinhood in March, when the stock was still trading near $85 per share. Robinhood has been one of the most successful positions in my portfolio, and while I have largely encouraged selling off large-cap momentum winners this year, Robinhood is one of the exceptions I plan to retain in my portfolio. The company's growth potential is evident, especially with the depth of the company's innovation roadmap: it has plenty of avenues to expand both in the U.S. and overseas as it takes market share from both incumbent banks and brokerages. At the same time, the company is starting to flex its profitability potential, with adjusted EBITDA surging. I'm reiterating my buy opinion here.

Before we get into Robinhood's latest Q2 successes that drove yet another spike in the stock, it's important for us to pause and reflect on Robinhood's consistently expanding roadmap. The chart below showcases the company's feature priorities for 2025:

To me, Robinhood is spreading its proverbial tentacles wide. In launching Robinhood Legend this year (with advanced charting and trading capabilities), the company is broadening itself to active traders. Internationally, the company launched its brokerage to 30 countries in the EU this year, building on top of its successful UK launch - and entering into a market with a very fragmented brokerage landscape. And on top of this, the company continues to build on its banking products for all of its customers: the company is set to release new "private banking" features, including estate and tax planning, for its Robinhood Gold customers later in Fall 2025.

As Robinhood scales into more services and more markets, the company is capable of generating significant economies of scale. The company is already on a clip rate of ~$0.5 billion in adjusted EBITDA per quarter, which is pitched to grow as Robinhood expands its slate of products.

Stay long here: Robinhood's momentum is still heating up.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Robinhood's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 revenue trends are shown below:

Robinhood's revenue surged 45% y/y to $989 million, decimating Wall Street's expectations of $913 million (+33% y/y) by a twelve-point margin. Also, the company's overall top-line growth rate largely kept pace with 50% y/y growth in Q1.

The chart below, meanwhile, zooms into Robinhood's transactional revenue: the spread it earns from trading, which represents just over half of the company's overall haul. Here, the biggest drivers of growth are crypto and options. The surge in crypto interest, especially after the establishment of a U.S. crypto reserve essentially legitimized Bitcoin, has led to a flurry in trading interest as crypto market caps have surged. Crypto revenue grew 98% y/y to $160 million for Robinhood. Investors' added risk appetite has also poured money into options, which grew 46% y/y to $265 million. And while it's still a relatively small portion of overall revenue, Robinhood has also waded into events contracts since the tail end of last year (betting on sporting outcomes and elections), which positions Robinhood not only to take market share from banks and brokerages but also from the likes of DraftKings (DKNG) and FanDuel.

Meanwhile, Robinhood also continues to power growth via its net interest income, which will become an increasingly important function of the company's growth and profitability as Robinhood shifts into a banking super-app that earns interest from deposits. Net interest income grew 25% y/y to $357 million. In particular, the growth in the company's margin balances has continued to power net interest income and represents over a tenth of Robinhood's overall revenue.

Why this is critical is because Robinhood can continue to earn attractive spreads on margin even as rates fall (which will diminish the company's earnings from corporate cash and cash sweep).

Fueling deposits and engagement in Robinhood is the company's dizzying array of products. Relatively new launches, like the Gold Card and retirement accounts, are seeing their membership bases multiply on a year-over-year basis. Per CEO Vlad Tenev's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

Robinhood Strategies. We've grown that by multiples, now over 100,000 funded customers and over $0.5 billion in assets. So just a few months after launch there. We've tripled Robinhood Gold cardholders year-to-date. So over 300,000 cardholders. We continue to like what we're seeing, and we're going to keep accelerating the rollout from here.

Retirement assets are now over $20 billion, over $20 billion in assets. So this is customers entrusting us with their most long- term serious money. And that's more than doubled in the past year.

Of course, the most important metric for Robinhood's growth is that customers continue to steer deposits into their Robinhood accounts. As most investors are aware, Robinhood posts its key metrics on a monthly basis. After the close of the second quarter, we note that monthly deposits in the month of July (the first month of Q3) spiked 28% y/y to $6.4 billion, representing nearly double the deposits relative to the prior two months.

The company's margin book also grew by ~6% to $9.5 billion since the end of Q2, while crypto trading volumes also surged 217% y/y in July, which is the precursor to a very strong Q3 ahead.

On the profitability front, Robinhood's adjusted EBITDA grew 82% y/y to $549 million:

As Robinhood unleashes a dizzying array of new products, it's not spending lavishly on product development or sales; it's also driving economies of scale, with adjusted EBITDA margins improving 12 points y/y.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $109, Robinhood trades at a market cap of $97.15 billion. After we net off the $4.33 billion of cash on the company's latest balance sheet, Robinhood's resulting enterprise value is $92.82 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26, Wall Street analysts are currently expecting Robinhood to generate $4.77 billion in revenue, or 18% y/y growth. If we assume a 53% adjusted EBITDA margin on that revenue profile (consistent with the company's TTM margins, assuming no further expansion as interest rate declines will put pressure on top-line growth), adjusted EBITDA would be $2.53 billion.

This positions Robinhood's valuation at 36.7x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. I do view Robinhood's steep valuation multiples as one of the key risks that the company faces. Valuation risk is also joined by several fundamental risks. Today, the company is enjoying tailwinds from both heightened market volatility (fueling very active trading in crypto and options) as well as high interest rates. Trading volumes may subside, and interest rates are all but certain to drop, which will drive deceleration in Robinhood's growth rate.

That being said, deceleration is already baked into consensus estimates (decelerating from 45% revenue growth in Q2 to a mid-teens growth rate next year). And though Robinhood's valuation is steep, the company has a vast opportunity to offset natural deceleration simply as it expands into more markets outside of the U.S. and broadens its offering to include a full suite of banking services.

Stay long here: in my view, there are still more tailwinds than headwinds on the horizon for Robinhood.

data.title: BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

The MSCI World Index gained 11.47% in the second quarter; however, these strong gains mask the heightened volatility throughout the period due to tariff policy uncertainties. Announced U.S. trade policies triggered a global equity sell-off in April, driven by concerns of potential trade wars, rising consumer prices, and the heightened risks of a broader economic slowdown. However, markets recovered in the second half of the quarter as investors welcomed a de-escalation in trade tensions, along with strong corporate earnings reports, resilient consumer spending data, and accommodative central bank policies.

“We expect continued uncertainty, and, consequently, volatility, in the equity markets due to the dynamic nature of tariff policy.”

At the beginning of April, President Trump announced sweeping tariffs on America’s trade partners, stating a goal to lower America’s trade imbalances and achieve bilateral trade deficits of zero. Specifically, these policies would include tariffs on China at 34%, the European Union (E.U.) at 20%, and Asian trade partners, such as Vietnam, Japan, Taiwan, and South Korea (with tariffs ranging from 20-46%). For countries where the U.S. runs a trade surplus, a baseline 10% tariff was announced. Investors were shocked and rattled; stocks sold off as the magnitude of these tariffs were far more pervasive than initially anticipated. A week later, a 90-day pause was instituted, and a universal 10% baseline tariff was enacted to allow for further negotiations.

The only country excluded from the 90-day pause was China, which saw a 145% tariff applied (a level reached through a series of escalations); China responded by levying a 125% tariff on American-made goods. After trade representatives from the U.S. and China met in May, the U.S. trimmed Chinese tariffs from 145% to 30% and China cut its tariffs on American-made goods from 125% to 10%. This de-escalation between the world’s two largest economies set the stage for further negotiations and drove a rebound in global equity performance. As similar patterns emerged in trade negotiations with countries across Asia, the E.U., Canada, and elsewhere, global equity markets rallied, and market participants expected that trade deals with the U.S. were ultimately achievable, despite the Trump administration’s tough initial stance.

Trade tensions between the U.S. and the E.U. also flared up during the quarter. President Trump threatened a 50% tariff on imports from the E.U., on top of the already announced 10% baseline tariff, and then quickly paused its implementation to allow for negotiations. America’s tariff announcements would likely challenge growth prospects for an already stagnant Europe. In response, the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and other major central banks of Europe cut rates to ease credit conditions. With these cuts, the central banks of Europe signaled the shift of their collective focus from fighting inflation to supporting economic growth.

During the first quarter earnings season, corporations reported better-than-expected earnings growth and addressed key investor themes, including the consumer outlook, continued artificial intelligence (AI) investments, and strategies to navigate a dynamic tariff environment. Overall, consumer spending remained strong and undeterred by the myriad of tariff threats, which drove earnings growth. However, companies have issued more cautious outlooks and have paused investment plans. Many are opting for a “wait-and-see” approach, preferring finalized trade deals to be signed before implementing price adjustments or reorganizing supply chains.

The large technology companies within the Index reported strong earnings and continue to spend on AI infrastructure. These tech giants reported continued demand for their AI hardware and services, which was driven by efficiency gains and product improvements in the quarter. Consequently, they issued better-than-expected guidance and higher capital expenditure plans to expand AI computing capacity in the year ahead, which drove equity gains and positive sentiment.

The Index recovered from the tariff shocks, but uncertainty related to global trade policies continues to weigh on investor sentiment. Geopolitical risk re-entered the conversation as conflict between Israel and Iran erupted, but the two nations quickly agreed to a ceasefire. This caused volatility in the price of crude oil globally, but didn’t derail the stock gains that were driven by tariff de-escalation and strong earnings.

Top 10 Holdings (6/30/25)

The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a security. Large concentrations can increase share price volatility.

PERFORMANCE REVIEW

The Fund trailed the Index in the period, driven by a negative stock selection effect. Positive stock selection effects across the industrials and communication services sectors supported relative performance. An overweight allocation in the information technology sector contributed to a positive allocation effect which overshadowed a smaller negative selection effect to support relative performance.

Conversely, negative stock selection effects across the consumer discretionary and financials sectors detracted from relative results. Within the health care sector, the Fund’s overweight positioning led to a negative allocation effect that detracted from relative performance.

TOP CONTRIBUTORS

The top contributors to relative performance include Microsoft, NVIDIA, BAE Systems, Amazon.com, and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM).

TOP DETRACTORS

The top detractors from relative performance include UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Chevron (CVX), Apple, LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), and ICON (ICLR).

MARKET OUTLOOK

Fayez Sarofim & Co.’s investment philosophy remains steadfast during periods of heightened uncertainty. While we expect volatility to continue as markets await further clarity on the potential impacts of tariffs and the direction of monetary and fiscal policies, we have conviction in the long-term strength of the capital markets. We believe that our portfolio companies, which we believe are comprised of global industry leaders, are well-equipped to navigate near- term market challenges.

We will continue to stay vigilant and invest in accordance with our long-standing investment philosophy, as we have done during previous periods of uncertainty. We believe long-term value is driven by companies that are in dominant global market-leading businesses in structurally attractive industries operating in multiple geographies, led by skilled management teams capable of achieving resilient, sustainable earnings growth.

PERFORMANCE SUMMARY

The BNY Mellon Worldwide Growth Fund underperformed its benchmark, the MSCI World Index, in the second quarter of 2025.

Share price and investment return fluctuate, and an investor's shares may be worth more or less than original cost upon redemption. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. Performance for periods less than 1 year is not annualized. The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus, or a summary prospectus, if available, that contains this and other information about a fund, contact your financial professional. For more information, call 1-800-373-9387 or visit bny.com/investments. Read the prospectus carefully before investing. Investors should discuss with their financial professional the eligibility requirements for Class I shares, which are available only to certain eligible investors, and the historical results achieved by the fund’s respective share classes.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Risks

Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks to varying degrees. Small and mid-sized company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than larger company stocks as these companies are less established and more volatile earnings histories. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries. A concentration of companies in a narrow sector could cause performance to be more volatile than funds invested in a broader range of industries.

Index Definitions

The MSCI World Index is designed to measure global equity performance of developed markets. The Index includes 23 MSCI national developed market indices. Investors cannot invest directly in any index.

Definitions

NAV is Net Asset Value. YTD is Year to Date. FDIC is Federal Deposit Insurance Corp.

Artificial intelligence (AI) refers to computer systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision-making, and language translation.

As of 6/30/25 the companies mentioned represented 36.63% of the fund’s portfolio in the aggregate. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a particular security. Other holdings may not have performed as well as some of those listed herein. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

This material has been provided for informational purposes only and should not be construed as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular investment product, strategy, investment manager or account arrangement, and should not serve as a primary basis for investment decisions. Prospective investors should consult a legal, tax or financial professional in order to determine whether any investment product, strategy or service is appropriate for their particular circumstances. Views expressed are those of the author stated and do not reflect views of other managers or the firm overall. Views are current as of the date of this publication and subject to change. This information contains projections or other forward-looking statements regarding future events, targets or expectations, and is only current as of the date indicated. There is no assurance that such events or expectations will be achieved, and actual results may be significantly different from that shown here. The information is based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate and may be superseded by subsequent market events or for other reasons. References to specific securities, asset classes and financial markets are for illustrative purposes only and are not intended to be and should not be interpreted as recommendations. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission.

data.title: DSV A/S: M&A Powerhouse Faces Huge Schenker Integration Task
data.description:
DSV A/S (OTCPK:DSDVF) (OTCPK:DSDVY), the largest 3PL provider in the world after the acquisition of Schenker, gained more than 18%, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 7.1% gain in line with my Strong Buy rating. The company has yet to reach my price target, but the stock price development has been encouraging in a complex market. In this report, I discuss the Q2 2025 earnings and update my price target.

DSV A/S Is Navigating Several Dynamics

First and foremost, when analyzing DSV A/S, we should note that the company is navigating several complex dynamics. The first is the macroeconomic picture, where trade can affect trade lanes and induce currency fluctuations.

Secondly, DSV A/S has been working on optimizing its own business, cutting lower gross margin business, which can affect the company’s volumes. The third element is the integration of Schenker. Until that integration is completed, DSV A/S will incur integration costs. For 2025, the company expects DKK 2-2.5 billion in transaction and integration-related costs, with the integration expected to be completed by 2028. Parallel with the integration, DSV A/S is also tasked with improving the financial performance of Schenker. Part of the M&A rationale for DSV A/S is to acquire other logistics providers and bring their EBIT margins up to the standard of DSV A/S. According to the P&L for Q2 2025, DSV A/S (including M&A) has margins of around 8% compared to 4% for Schenker. So, there is a lot to do in terms of bringing those margins up to standard, and that also provides an opportunity for the stock.

At the same time, DSV A/S also has some weak spots, particularly in Europe, just like Schenker, where the business combination could drive performance.

DSV A/S Sees Good Performance In Air and Sea, Offset by Challenging Road and Solutions Market Conditions

For the second quarter, revenue growth was driven by the acquisition of Schenker. Excluding the acquisition, DSV A/S would have reported a 1.1% decline in sales. Gross profits improved 2.4% despite the decline in organic sales. EBIT before special items declined 4.6%, but including inorganic contributions jumped over 15%.

DSV A/S saw an increase of 8.8% in inorganic gross profit growth for its Air operations and 9.5% for its Sea operations, bringing the inorganic gross profit growth to 9.2% and 9.4% growth in EBIT. Including M&A, gross profits increased 40% while EBIT grew 19%, reflecting transaction and integration costs.

Air volumes excluding DSV A/S declined slightly but yields were up 12% on constant currency. DSV A/S is cutting volumes with lower margin, and we saw that reflected in the company’s volumes and yield changes. Those changes were primarily from perishables and some retail volumes.

Sea Freight volumes increased 43% and 2% excluding M&A with a 7% constant currency improvement excluding Schenker, largely offset by forex impacts.

The Road business booked total sales growth of 96% but with an inorganic sales decline of 4.1%. Gross profit declined 7.5% organically and 28.8% decline inorganically.

EBIT margins dropped from 5.2% to 2.5%, largely reflecting the low margins that Schenker has on its Road business. Somewhat interesting to note is that while Schenker has some issues with its road business in Germany, the US, the UK, and Norway, DSV did not provide a lot of insight on the significant performance decline on an organic basis. It only noted challenging market conditions in Germany.

The Contract Logistics business saw its organic performance tumble by 15% on sales and 41.6% on EBIT, driven by lower utilization and a higher cost base for new warehouses. This is actually where Schenker did contribute, as its EBIT margins of 8% are above the reported EBIT margin of the business combination.

DSV A/S Stock Is Now A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For DSV A/S, the fundamentally supported price target has increased from $291.37 to $356.56 per share, largely driven on the assumption that the company will not issue stock to accelerate debt repayment through issuing shares. The company targets a 2x leverage by H1 2027, and under the current projections, that would easily be reached, leaving room for the company to recommence share repurchases. The integration of Schenker and maturing the profits do provide a risk, while we saw DSV’s organic business showing some weak spots. As a result, I am adjusting my price target to $287.86, indicating 24% upside. I am also modifying my strong buy rating to buy driven by DSV’s organic business. We also do note that the share price is subject to the company’s decision on how it wants to address its debt and the integration costs.

Conclusion: DSV A/S Is Attractive But Schenker Integration Is A Risk

I do believe that DSV A/S could provide a compelling investment opportunity, but at the same time I also believe that we should take note of the cost related to the Schenker integration, Schenker’s weak margins, and the challenges DSV has faced in its existing business. I would not want to hold Schenker’s weaker margins against DSV, as maturing those margins is why DSV acquired the company in the first place, but I do believe that adjusting the target and rating to align with the risk and timeline of integration is fair.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500: You're Less Diversified Than You Think, These ETFs Prove It
data.description:
This article has a lot of charts in it. But it is not my oft-used technical analysis. It is about what I think could be the biggest error of omission (or commission) that long-term S&P 500 investors are making. And what's worse, it keeps padding the pockets of Wall Street salespeople, at the expense of mainstream investors.

Why isn't this more obvious? Think about it: who in the traditional investment business, where fees are based on assets under management, be they investment advisors or fund firms, would call out something that is against their best interests?

Well, a fiduciary would. I was one for 27 years before selling my practice and retiring from that aspect of the investment world in 2020. 5 years later, I've had a great opportunity to interact with a far more diverse investor set than when I was happily investing in and servicing a few dozen wealthy families. The past decade has seen a dramatic increase in the information made available to "retail" investors, to make their own decisions.

Yet I don't see much press about the thesis here: that literally trillions of dollars invested in S&P 500 index funds are not nearly as diversified as many holders think. Because, as it turns out, the top 20-50 stocks essentially "run the joint" known as the S&P 500.

Why bother with the rest? The allure of diversification (that doesn't exist to the extent many believe) is one factor. So too is the herd mentality. Owning an S&P 500 index ETF like SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:SPY) is akin to a status symbol now.

That has also led to a familiar Wall Street phenomenon. Provide what are essentially 15 flavors of chocolate ice cream, that all taste substantially similar. But put enough marketing dollars and media muscle behind it so that it becomes so popular, few will notice. In fact, they will be prone to buying more and more products, most of which are similar, and which add no "alpha" for them. But to product providers and advisors, what counts is not that investors are diversified. As long as they THINK they are.

Hey, it's buyer beware. And I can't put myself in the position of any individual investor. I just like to know what I own. That includes not only what it is, but what it is not. I trade SPY and many other S&P 500 ETFs, and I invest a portion of my long-term portfolio in the broad index. That is in large part because often use options around it (puts below, calls for more upside, or collars to secure a best- and worst-case outcome). There, the goal is S&P 500 exposure with liquidity, and SPY has the most liquid options.

If someone is just trying to get market exposure, the S&P 500 in its traditional form works just fine, up to a certain dollar level. But once the portfolio size increases, to 6 digits and then 7 and 8, the pennies are no longer pennies, so to speak.

The bigger concern is not realizing that the S&P 500 index is really more like the S&P 50 index, for all intents and purposes. So once we start looking at what we actually own in an index fund, we realize that most stocks are non-contributors, in up and down markets. And as I see it, that means a lot of stocks (more than 400 of the S&P 500) are likely being dragged up and down with their sectors, industries or the full index itself.

This is not about "good or bad," but about understanding what we own. And in expressing my view, for public discourse, that in my own portfolio, I am being VERY selective about the stocks I will own individually. Now, more than ever, the "default" is to consider a stock to be non-alpha-generating most of the time.

That puts the onus on a stock to truly be a potential source of alpha when I own it. Otherwise, I am better off with either the index, or one of the more focused versions below. And just as importantly, if I want to perform like the index, or a bit ahead over time, I don't need 500 stocks in my possession to do it. I can focus on perhaps 30-50 of them.

OK, enough theory concerning my views. Let's look at the evidence. It is strong.

Here's a 3-year rolling return chart of SPY, as well as ETFs that track the top 200, 100 and 50 within SPY. They are all capitalization weighted, so they all work the same way. They just stop adding stocks after the amount shown in the ETF name.

That chart above shows a very long period of time in which no one of these "different" ETFs has created much consistent daylight versus the others. That tells me that 50 stocks are doing the work of 500. Sure, you can just own the slightly lower expense ratio of SPY or its direct peers. But don't fool yourself into thinking you are diversified across 500 stocks. Or even half that number.

When we look at maximum peak to trough drawdown, with 1-year lookback, shown below, we see that, to everyone's surprise, there's a huge gap. OK, JUST KIDDING. Even when stress tested in this way, there's not enough of a gap between the top 50 stocks and all 500 to confirm that the other 450 add much value. Maybe if you are a multi-billion dollar institution. But not we retail investors and perhaps boutique investment advisors and managers.

Let's focus even more: the top 20 S&P 500 stocks

As it turns out, iShares apparently figured this out too. Because last year, they introduced the iShares Top 20 U.S. Stocks ETF (TOPT). There used to be a popular "major market index" which was 20 stocks, but in this era of S&P 500 love, no one remembers it. This may be the closest thing to it in an ETF format. And I like it a lot. Not for the options access, since SPY is still prohibitively better for that.

But as I see it, if one has enough money (and it doesn't take that much) to own even the top 20 stocks outright, they can form their own S&P 500 clone of sorts. And from there, add/reduce weights of the stocks versus their index weights, and even add in a dollop of the others, or the equal weighted S&P 500 (RSP). There are many ways to outperform the S&P 500, and with some option work attached (namely SPY puts), that outperformance on the downside can be dramatic. I've lived the latter part of that statement throughout my career, as the guy who bends but doesn't break in suddenly awful markets.

However, going forward, I want to be even more competitive on the upside than I have been in the past. I've spent many hours updating my investing group on my thought process. And while I'm always thinking through it, adjusting to the markets and what works, and editing the process and guardrails, I think this thesis has "legs." That is, SPY and its clones are not the only way to invest in the long-term success of "the S&P 500."

There's a lot more to it, and I plan to dive deeper into this in future articles here. Included in that will be a deeper look at TOPT, and probably XLG as well.

data.title: Credit Spreads At Historical Lows, TLT Vs. VCLT
data.description:
The purpose of this article is to provide a brief update on current market conditions and specifically credit spreads between U.S. investment-grade bonds and U.S. Treasuries. We will cover the following ETFs:

  • iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (NASDAQ:TLT)
  • iShares 10-20 Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLH)
  • Vanguard Long-Term Corporate Bond Index Fund (NASDAQ:VCLT)
  • iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond (LQD)

ETFs Metrics

LQD holdings have a weighted average maturity of 12.76 years and an average yield to maturity of 5.14%. The 12-month trailing dividend yield of the ETF is 4.45%

TLH holdings have a weighted average maturity of 16.81 years and an average yield to maturity of 4.83%. The 12-month trailing dividend yield of the ETF is 4.20%

VCLT portfolio has an effective maturity of 22.2 years, and a yield to maturity of 5.8%. The 12-month trailing dividend of the fund is 5.51%

TLT portfolio has a weighted average maturity of 16.81 years and an average yield to maturity of 4.83%. The 12-month trailing dividend is 4.45%.

The reason for this article is that the spread between IG bonds and U.S. Treasuries has recently collapsed to 75 basis points, the lowest since June 2008.

This shows confidence in the U.S. corporation's ability to repay debt. The narrower the spread is, the less investors demand a higher risk premium, and companies take full advantage of that.

Below, we have posted two charts comparing the ETFs from above, and a wide gap is clearly visible.

Our software even better illustrates this. The most significant gap is between TLT and VCLT.

It displays that TLT and VCLT have a strong historical correlation of approximately 90%, but recently, VCLT has deviated from the benchmark TLT. Such deviations are normalized in the long term.

In terms of duration, TLT and VCLT are very similar, but while the VCLT portfolio is of U.S. investment-grade bonds, TLT holds U.S. Treasuries. U.S. debt might have been downgraded, but the U.S. still has the strongest economy in the world. I apologize for this being a subjective statement. It is the greatest country in the world.

How to position ourselves.

There are two ways, in our opinion, in which an intelligent income investor can benefit from the situation. The first way is to invest in investment-grade securities that are lagging their peers. The corporate bond rally, in contrast to the rally in equities, has been relatively broad, and securities that are late to the party eventually will catch up. Fundamentally, an argument can be made that the laggards are reasonably priced, but based on experience and technical analysis, they are more likely to catch up to their peers. VCLT is a hedging reaction here if there is no exit liquidity.

Another opportunity is Long TLT, Short VCLT. The gap will eventually narrow. We have not yet entered the arbitrage, but we are actively monitoring it. The upside of the arbitrage is that it will most certainly be successful in the future, and the borrowing costs are relatively slim; however, the holding period cannot be determined at this moment, and if your capital allocation is limited, we advise caution.

Conclusion

There are always great investment opportunities when market sentiment shifts. Our job is not to predict the market but to invest intelligently. As for the tickers discussed, we rate them all HOLD for now. Such liquid instruments rarely suffer from short-term inefficiency compared to preferred stock or bonds, where a single seller or buyer could create a huge technical opportunity. Therefore, we would rather wait for either a wider gap or a market sentiment shift. With the repricing of the yield curve, we implore you to be extra vigilant about your portfolio and rebalance if needed. An income investor's long-term success requires a prepared hedging strategy even when the market seems favourable.

data.title: Automotive Properties: Acquisitions Will Boost Dividends
data.description:
Introduction

Automotive Properties REIT (APR.UN:CA) is an internally managed Canadian real estate investment trust focusing on the ownership of car dealership properties. As of the end of June, the company owned 80 properties that have been leased under long-term contracts with a weighted average lease term of 8.5 years (as of the end of Q2 2025). As it has been a while since I last discussed this real estate company, I figured this was a good time, considering it recently reported on its second quarter.

The Q2 results were satisfying, and this resulted in a distribution hike

As you already know, the net income result is not a good metric to judge the performance of a REIT, and the FFO and AFFO results provide a much better understanding of how a REIT is doing. I specifically like to use the AFFO for Canadian REITs, as this already includes the assumed capital expenditures.

As you can see below, Automotive Properties reported a net profit of 13 million Canadian dollars in the second quarter of this year, and this ultimately resulted in an FFO of C$12.8M. After also deducting C$125,000 for straight-line rent adjustments as well as just over C$100,000 for capital expenditures, the company reported a total adjusted funds from operations of approximately C$12.6M. On a per-share basis, this represents approximately C$0.256, an increase of roughly 7% compared to the second quarter of last year.

That's important because this means the payout ratio of distribution during Q2 2025 has dropped from 86% to just over 80%. And even looking at the financial results in the first six months of the year, we see a similar AFFO of around C$25M (up 7%) with a distribution coverage ratio of around 125%.

The strong performance allowed the REIT’s board to approve a 2.2% increase in the distribution rate. The newly announced monthly distribution is C$0.0685 per unit, which represents approximately C$0.822 per unit per year. Based on the current share price of C$11.89 per unit, this represents a dividend yield of 6.9%.

The distribution increase is also supported by the acquisition of seven new properties for a total of C$94M. The company is acquiring six assets in Quebec as well as a Rivian dealership in the United States.

I was originally aiming to see an AFFO per share of C$1.02-1.04 per unit in FY 2026, but I think these new acquisitions will allow me to increase my expectations, and I wouldn't be surprised to see a total AFFO/share of approximately C$1.10 per unit in FY 2027. This could also pave the way for additional dividend increases, and assuming a payout ratio of 82.5%, the distribution could increase to 90 or 91 cents per share (which could boost the distribution rate to just over 7.5%). And that's what makes this niche REIT attractive. The growth profile is quite interesting, especially if it can happen without too much dilution to the existing shareholders.

At the end of June, the debt ratio was approximately 44.4% of the Gross Book Value, and as the book value is based on a capitalization rate of 6.7%, the debt ratio appears to be quite decent, and the balance sheet could certainly handle additional acquisitions that are funded by debt rather than equity.

Investment thesis

I currently have no position in Automotive Properties, as I sold my stake after the run-up in 2024. That being said, I'm quite charmed by the REIT’s strong financial performance, the good distribution coverage ratio, and of course the recently increased dividend is quite helpful as well. It's also good to see the company continues to expand its asset portfolio, and as the interest rates on financial markets are going down in Canada, adding properties at a decent cap rate makes a lot of sense.

I have an excess amount of Canadian dollars kicking around (even after deploying some cash into Boardwalk REIT and Killam Apartment REIT), and I may initiate a long position in Automotive Properties within the next few weeks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Undervalued Pharma Heavyweight With Upside Potential
data.description:
Novo Nordisk (NYSE:NVO) is a Danish pharma heavyweight currently trading at undervalued levels, presenting an attractive opportunity for long-term investors. Together with Eli Lilly, the company has a dominant market position in the weight-loss treatment industry. NVO is a growing business operating at high margins.

Novo Nordisk shares fell by around 60% in the last 12 months, indicating a sell-off could be overdone. At a forward P/E of approximately 14, the company is trading around 40% cheaper than an average S&P 500 company, and not all are growing top and bottom-line figures over 20% in recent years.

The Danish healthcare giant operates in an industry expected to grow significantly in the years ahead; if the company maintains its dominant market share, upside is likely just from increased demand.

I think Novo Nordisk is a Strong Buy. It is one of the largest positions in my portfolio, and I think the stock could double in price over the next 12 to 18 months. Trading at undervalued levels, it presents an attractive opportunity, and with future tailwinds, I think the $110 stock price is not out of reach in the next 12 to 18 months.

Growth Of The Weight Loss Treatment Market Is The Key Tailwind

Novo Nordisk is addressing an important issue. A significant number of people in the world face overweight-related problems, and anti-obesity treatment has never been more in demand. The market had about $15 billion in sales in 2024, while Morgan Stanley has raised their guidance from $105 billion in 2035 to a staggering $150 billion.

If the weight loss market increases 10x, Novo Nordisk, as a market leader, will benefit significantly. Wegovy, the company's weight loss drug, has become synonymous with the treatment, and together with Eli Lilly's Zepbound is shaping the industry. The industry felt saturated with compounders offering alternatives due to supply shock, but with the issue resolved, only FDA-approved treatments will remain available in the market.

The National Institute of Health (NIH) shared statistics showing the trend in obesity and severe obesity, indicating a huge issue in the United States. 2017-2018 data suggested more than 42% of Americans were obese, with around 9% experiencing severe obesity.

Novo Nordisk is a well-known brand in the industry, which has an incredible 71% market share internationally. The company disclosed in its Q2 2025 earnings report that total class growth was around 30%, which is significant. With anti-obesity drugs becoming more popular and available worldwide, NVO's dominant 71% position leaves it well-positioned for long-term growth and success.

The US market share is not disclosed, but the company has reported that its total diabetes market share at around 32%. It is significant, as every third weight-loss drug sold came from Novo Nordisk.

Taking into account the growth outlook for the anti-obesity industry, Novo Nordisk should see significant upside driven by increased demand. The healthcare giant is investing heavily in R&D, implying current products could become more effective or new world-changing products could be introduced in the future.

Novo Nordisk is a market leader that continues to improve. With such a large market share internationally, the company is well-positioned for expected industry growth. It should be reflected in their top and bottom line numbers years ahead.

Oversold And Undervalued

The company is trading as a declining business, not a growth story. With a forward P/E of around 14, it is about 44% cheaper than the sector median and the average S&P 500 company. That is surprising and indicates undervaluation, as with top-line growth of 20.90%, it deserves a higher premium.

Novo Nordisk is expected to grow its revenue by about 17% next year, which would be 118% higher than the 8% industry average. If the market is pricing in saturation in the weight-loss treatment market or a loss of dominance, the expected growth does not support that. Forward EBITDA is expected to grow 18.57%, 130% higher than peers. These are impressive numbers, and the current valuation does not justify them — it feels like the stock is oversold, and weak healthcare sentiment may be bottoming.

If one thinks Novo is solely a growth story without significant bottom-line strength to support valuations, that's not true. EPS grew about 24% year over year, which is 49% higher than the industry average, and the bottom line is not expected to slow with projected EPS growth of 22% year-over-year for FY2025 at 3.86.

Novo Nordisk is growing its bottom line as successfully as its top line. Since 2020, the company has consistently increased profitability, and the current 6-month data suggest Novo is on its way to a strong, double-digit bottom-line growth.

NVO is operating in a high-margin business, which has grown consecutively over the past decade. Margins now appear to be consolidating around 34%, but with a large market share, it should be able to keep the momentum going in net income. On a TTM basis, the company reported nearly $17.5 billion in net income, which is already 20% higher than FY2024's $14 billion.

The company is oversold. The chart below suggests it is trading at historically low P/E levels, which indicates it may be positioned for a rebound. There are not that many companies in the market that could grow 20% annually, report double-digit bottom-line growth, and also trade at their 5-year P/E lows.

Nevertheless, the company is expected to grow its dividends by 13.31% next year, which is 3x compared to the industry's average. On a TTM basis, it grew its dividends by 17% — nearly 3x the sector median.

Novo Nordisk is a strong performer, with both top and bottom-line growth. The company is trading at undervalued levels, suggesting it may be a good time to acquire shares. I think Novo Nordisk will benefit a lot from the expected growth of the anti-obesity market, and while being a dominant force in the weight-loss treatment industry, investors may consider building a larger position while it's trading at historical lows.

Why I think Novo Nordisk May Reach $500 Billion Market Cap

NVO's current market cap is around $240 billion. It used to be around $640 billion in mid-2024, which implies a decline of more than 60%. To put it the perspective, the company's revenue grew more than 20% year-over-year since June 2024, from $9.7 billion to $12.1 billion.

Meanwhile, gross profit increased from about $8.3 billion to $10.1 billion, a nearly 19% rise. I think that the stock price drop doesn't make much sense — even if the markets are forward-looking, I think they mispriced the stock. Regardless of the risks and concerns, the company has been fundamentally growing.

There is a saying that markets can stay irrational longer than one can stay solvent — and that seems the case with Novo. More than half of the market cap has been erased over 12 months, while the company grew top and bottom lines by 20%.

To reach a $500 billion market cap — or my $110 price target — improved sentiment alone would be enough. I think it's irrational that the company is growing nearly twice the average S&P 500 company (20% to an average of 10%), is trading at nearly 50% lower multiple (25 compared to 14). This set-up is rare, and that's why I think it is an attractive opportunity for long-term investors. Thus, if the company were to trade at slightly above the average benchmark company, which would be reasonable because of the higher growth, at around a 28 forward multiple — at projected EPS of 3.90, the company would reach $110 price target.

This is realistic. A forward multiple of 28 would still be well below historical averages, and with the tailwinds ahead, I see no reason why it couldn't be trading at these levels in the future.

Wall Street analysts expect $4.00 EPS for FY2026. This supports my thesis and would require a lower multiple of 26.5x to reach the $110 target. However, I think the market is overly pessimistic — the company, in its recent quarterly announcement, guided operating profit to come at around 10% to 16% for FY2025.

Even though management didn't disclose projected EPS growth for FY2026, operating profit growth suggests analysts' EPS FY2026 estimates may be too conservative, as they imply just 3% bottom-line growth next year. The challenge of a saturated weight-loss market due to compounding products was already a risk last year, but the company still posted strong growth.

Novo Nordisk is a well-known brand, a dominant player in the high-margin weight-loss landscape, and is growing its top and bottom lines at nearly double the average benchmark company. With expectations of the anti-obesity market expanding to $125 billion at the midpoint by 2035, it would be enough for Novo to sustain its market share to benefit greatly from rising demand. While trading at historical lows, it presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors, and I think it's only a matter of time before sentiment improves.

Novo Nordisk Appears Undervalued Compared To Eli Lilly

Novo's main competition is another healthcare powerhouse, Eli Lilly. One might assume Novo Nordisk is struggling due to losing market share to LLY or its significant outperformance. However, that's not necessarily the case.

Weight-loss growth stories have faced their challenges over the past year, and concerns around compounding have put pressure on both stocks. Eli Lilly has lost about 25% of its market cap over 12 months, and while it has outperformed Novo, the decline in its stock price has been significant. Both companies largely underperformed the index.

While comparing the companies fundamentally, valuation metrics suggest Novo Nordisk is significantly more attractive as an investment compared to Eli Lilly. For instance, the price-to-cash-flow on a TTM basis indicates that Novo is nearly 5x more undervalued than its American competitor. Realizing that Novo dominates more than two-thirds of the international market segment, that is a huge leverage against LLY.

Metric Novo Nordisk Eli Lilly
P/E GAAP (FWD) 14.07 31.94
EV/Sales (FWD) 5.05 10.84
EV/EBITDA (FWD) 10.38 24.34
Price/Cash Flow (TTM) 12.49 57.90
Dividend Yield (TTM) 3.18% 0.82%

From a profitability perspective, Novo Nordisk appears to be a more attractive investment choice, too. It is operating at a higher gross profit margin than Eli Lilly (about 84% to 82%), while both net income margin and cash from operations indicate Novo's stronger positioning.

Metric Novo Nordisk Eli Lilly
Gross Profit Margin (TTM) 84.26% 82.64%
Net Income Margin (TTM) 35.61% 25.91%
Cash From Operations (TTM) $19.19 billion $10.94 billion

Comparing the two, it's not necessarily Eli Lilly's fault, but its presence adds pressure to Novo's stock. Although I think that LLY is a great investment opportunity for long-term investors, Novo, from a fundamental analysis perspective, appears more undervalued and suggests the downside for its stock price could be more limited compared to its American peer.

Main Risks And Concerns

Despite being highly undervalued, there are a few risks and concerns we should take into consideration. They could affect the stock price, especially in the near term.

In its recent Q2 report, management has cut the guidance for total sales and operating growth. Sales growth was lowered from the expected 13-21% to 8%-14%, while operating growth was expected to be between 16-24% initially, but was lowered to 10-16%. It resulted in a significant drop in the stock price — and if the guidance were cut again, uncertainty could pressure the stock price further.

Contraction in profit margins. For a while now, margins have been consolidating, which presents a risk that they might have peaked. With potential alternatives to Novo's products, the company may be forced to lower prices, which would affect margins.

R&D challenges. To scale anti-obesity treatment, healthcare giants are working to replace injectable drugs with oral products. If LLY succeeds first, Novo could face market share losses, especially in the US, accelerating the sell-off.

Regulatory concerns are always an open risk to healthcare companies. Changes within the weight-loss industry framework could bring uncertainty, and forward-looking markets could pressure dominant players.

The broader stock market has experienced a significant rally from its April lows, which could signal a temporary pause or consolidation. If that happens and sentiment shifts to profit-taking, Novo's stock price could decline alongside the market.

I think investors should watch whether Novo Nordisk can continue growing both the top and bottom lines. If the company succeeds, it presents an incredible opportunity for long-term investors, and purchasing stock at historical lows could potentially lead to significant benchmark outperformance.

Conclusion: Novo Nordisk Is A Strong Buy — A Stock To Hold For Years

I think that Novo Nordisk at current levels is an absolute buy. Therefore, I state a Strong Buy rating with a $110 price target over 12 to 18 months. If the company were to trade at a slight premium compared to a typical S&P 500 company at 25, the price target could even appear conservative.

The business operates in a high-margin landscape and is growing significantly faster than the sector norm. It is highly profitable, consistently invests in R&D, and keeps improving its products. The weight-loss market is expected to increase nearly 10x by 2035, and with 71% international market share, Novo would be one of the main beneficiaries.

11 analysts suggest a one-year average price of $63.57, implying about 15% growth. I am more optimistic — I think if anti-obesity market sentiment improves, it may be enough to drive a significant upside. However, based on the historical growth and management's FY2025 guidance, I think the company will continue expanding its top and bottom lines — and if that is reassured, the market should reward investors. Novo Nordisk has strong tailwinds ahead, making it a compelling investment possibility to consider.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Nvidia Time
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The chipmaker’s numbers could signal if the AI trade still has legs. (0:17) Post-Jackson Hole, the Fed’s favorite inflation gauge hits. (1:54) European post offices pause package shipments to the U.S. (3:19)

The following is an abridged transcript:

The 800 lb. earnings gorilla hoves into view this week as Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) reports earnings on Wednesday.

The consensus for the biggest company on Wall Street is for EPS of $1.01 on revenue of $46 billion. The temporary ban on H20 processors to China that took effect in April is expected to have just a minimal impact on quarterly performance. The Trump administration recently removed the ban in exchange for a 15% slice of sales revenue, which Oppenheimer expects to be offset by price increases.

SA analyst KM Capital, which has a Strong Buy on the stock, says reasons for bullishness ahead of earnings “include accelerating AI tailwinds as well as Nvidia's dominance in the industry. Moreover, Nvidia is a real earnings surprise champion that very rarely misses consensus forecasts.”

The recent pullback “looks like a prime opportunity to buy NVDA before earnings,” they said.

But SA analyst Bears of Wall Street says “here’s a possibility that we’re in the middle of the AI bubble since the capital returns by hyperscalers on AI hardware might yield limited returns, while the current record rate of capital expenditures on AI infrastructure is unsustainable.”

“Considering Nvidia’s great exposure to a handful of customers that generate a significant portion of its sales, there’s a risk that if the bubble pops soon, then the company’s growth opportunities will be limited, while its shares might collapse from the current levels,” they said.

Along with Nvidia, 14 other S&P 500 companies will report earnings this week.

On the calendar:

  • Bank of Montreal (BMO), MongoDB (MDB) and Okta (OKTA) report numbers on Tuesday.
  • Along with Nvidia, Crowdstrike (CRWD), Snowflake (SNOW) and HP (HPQ) weigh in on Wednesday.
  • On Thursday, Dell (DELL), Dollar General (DG), Li Auto (LI), Ulta Beauty (ULTA) and Best Buy (BBY) issue numbers.
  • Alibaba (BABA) reports Friday.

Looking to the economy, traders are adjusting to the post-Jackson Hole macro environment.

Following Fed Chairman Jay Powell’s speech on Friday odds of a September quarter-point rate cut rebounded and are now at 75% (lower than they were immediately post-Powell). Another cut of 25 bps is priced in through the end of the year, with a one-in-four chance of 50 bps of cuts in total.

Dario Perkins, managing director of global macro at T.S. Lombard, says: "Surprising to see Powell offer such a clear policy signal with so much data before the next FOMC meeting (and yes, was a clear signal)."

Neil Dutta, head of economics at Renaissance Macro Research, says: "This is about the September Fed meeting. If you get solid economic data, you’ll get a hawkish cut in September. If you get bad data, you’ll get a dovish cut.”

“That’s how to think about this. Through the lens of an insurance cut being green lit in September. Powell did not lay the ground work for a series of rate cuts.”

This Friday, the Fed will get the latest measure of its favorite inflation gauge, the core PCE price index. That arrives with the July income and spending figures.

Economists are expecting a rise of 0.3%. That would boost the annual rate to 2.9%, the highest since February.

Wells Fargo economists say: “Tariff-related price pressures are broadening across the goods sector and appear to be spilling over into the services sector.”

“We ultimately expect core PCE inflation to peak slightly above 3% by the end of the year,” they said. “With inflation drifting in the wrong direction and the labor market losing momentum, the Federal Reserve faces difficult trade-offs in balancing its dual mandate.”

In the news this weekend, lack of clarity on rules about package exemptions is leading to a pause on shipping of packages by a host of European nations to the U.S.

The “de minimus” exemption allows packages worth less than $800 to come into the U.S. duty free. It is set to expire on Friday, August 29.

Postal services in Germany, Denmark, Sweden and Italy said on Saturday they will stop shipping most merchandise to the U.S. effective immediately. France and Austria will follow on Monday. The U.K.'s Royal Mail said it would halt shipments to the U.S. on Tuesday to allow time for those packages to arrive before duties kick in.

Spotify (SPOT) plans further price increases for customers as it invests in new features and pursues a goal of 1 billion users, according to a senior executive.

Alex Norström, co-president and chief business officer, told the Financial Times that price rises were “part of our toolbox now” after many years of keeping them flat.

Spotify is developing features to provide more value and “stickiness” for subscribers. The company recently added a way to customize transitions between tracks on its nearly 9 billion user-created playlists. It has also expanded its services to include audiobooks and podcasts, as well as tools like an AI DJ.

And for income investors, Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and S&P Global (SPGI) go ex-dividend on Tuesday. J&J pays out on Sept. 9 and S&P Global pays out on Sept. 10.

Hyatt (H) goes ex-dividend on Wednesday, paying out on Sept. 10.

Tyson Foods (TSN) goes ex-dividend on Friday, with a Sept. 12 payout date.

data.title: JPLD: Continues To Be Attractive In Today's Macro Environment
data.description:
Thesis

In the beginning of the year, we started our coverage of the JPMorgan Limited Duration Bond ETF (JPLD) with a 'Buy' rating. The name has performed since, with a total return in excess of 3.63% since. Given the shift in the macro landscape, we are going to revisit the fund and its analytics and outline why we believe the ETF to still be a solid 'Buy'.

Some analytics first

  • AUM: $2.1 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.24%
  • Annualized volatility: 1.85%
  • SEC yield: 4.41%

The name has seen a significant increase in its AUM in the past year:

Since August 2024, the fund has seen a 3x increase in its total assets under management to $2.18 billion currently. That is a very significant move, and indicates investor interest in the product.

Composition - overweight agency mortgages

The ETF is currently overweight agency mortgages, followed by ABS securities:

  • Agency mortgage: 29%
  • ABS: 23%
  • CMBS: 15%
  • Non-agency MBS: 8.5%
  • CLO: 8%
  • Credit: 7%
  • Treasury: 6%
  • Other: residual

The fund has a rate-focused profile, and runs a short duration of 1.8 years, with an average life for its portfolio of 3 years. Targeting the short end of the curve makes this ETF a play on Fed Funds. JPLD is set to gain in value as the Fed cuts rates since the front end of the yield curve has significant correlation to Fed Funds.

The name is very active, with a portfolio turnover rate of 30%, and a very high quality collateral composition:

  • AAA: 73%
  • AA: 13%
  • A: 10%
  • BBB: 4%

The fund therefore takes very little in terms of credit risk, which explains its ultra-low volatility. An investor can almost see this fund as a cash parking vehicle with a duration aspect to it.

Comparison versus its cohort

The fund is the JPMorgan answer to the BlackRock's Short Duration Bond Active ETF (NEAR):

We have also put in the comparison cohort the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC) and the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY). Most of the names here are also overweight agency MBS bonds.

We can see a very similar performance in the cohort in the past year, with a low dispersion in total returns and similar volatility. BINC has had the largest drawdown in the contemplated time period, with a significant move down in April 2025.

In essence, all the above names represent take-on rates via their overweight positioning in Agency MBS, and also short duration vehicles set to gain as the Fed cuts rates. There is an active management aspect to all of them, with managers trying to generate additional yield via actively trading securities and the portfolios.

Macro context

While the market is still debating how many cuts will happen in 2025 and if Powell will be ousted, one thing is certain for 2026, namely lower rates:

The above are the market traded probabilities for rate cuts. The blue boxes indicate where Fed Funds are most likely to end up per Fed meeting given where the market trades futures currently. One thing is for certain, and that is lower Fed Funds by 100 bps in 2026. As we can see from the able above, the blue boxes for mid-2026 are squarely in the 325-250 bps range. Also kindly remember that the current Fed chair mandate expires in May 2026, and President Trump will nominate another chair. It is not a secret for anybody that President Trump wants much lower rates, so one can only assume the new chair will have a similar view on the topic.

Lower rates are coming with a 1-year horizon, although the market is still debating the timing. JPLD is a fund that will benefit directly from lower Fed Funds via its composition and duration profile.

Risk factors

As we have seen from the fund composition, there is very little credit risk here. Even if a recession comes, JPLD should not really suffer a drawdown higher than -1.5%. The main risk, in our view, is if rates stay elevated. If that happens, the fund transforms itself into a dividend vehicle, and the 30-day SEC yield here is not all that appealing. The fund is to be bought with expectations of lower rates. So in a flat rate environment, the name might perform more in-line with money market funds. In our view, that is not a bad risk factor to have all considered.

Conclusion

JPLD is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name has a short duration of 1.8 years and represents a play on short rates via its overweight Agency MBS position. The fund runs almost no credit risk and has a very low annualized volatility that ends up sub 2%. The ETF has seen massive growth in AUM in the past year, now surpassing $2 billion in assets. The fund is set to benefit from lower Fed Funds and represent a very low-volatility way to take advantage of the coming monetary easing environment. We like JPLD and consider it to continue to be a 'Buy' in today's macro context.

data.title: Nvidia's Earnings Report Looks Like A Huge Gamble
data.description:
All eyes will turn to Nvidia (NVDA) this week, as investors hold their collective breath. For the most part, it is easy to argue that the health of the market is dependent on the health of Nvidia. The question isn't whether Nvidia will have solid results. The question is whether the results will be good enough to meet the high expectations, given its massive run-up.

However, based on the revenue and guidance history, as well as the current options positioning, playing this quarter's results looks like a huge gamble.

Revenue Trends

Revenue in the fiscal second quarter is expected to increase by 52.8% to $45.9 billion, with earnings rising by 47.7% to $1.00 per share, and gross margins of 72.1%. Analysts forecast fiscal third-quarter revenue to be $52.7 billion at the midpoint of the range, with margins expected to be 73.3%.

By now, everyone is probably aware that Nvidia tends to low-ball its guidance and then exceed the upper end of that range by $1 to $2 billion every quarter. Although last quarter, the beat was much smaller, at just $202 million.

We also know that the company aims for revenue to grow by $4 to $5 billion per quarter sequentially, which is something they have achieved since the third quarter of 2023. Although analysts expect the company to grow revenue by just $1.8 billion this quarter, compared to last quarter, it is expected to make up for that lack of growth in the fiscal third quarter by guiding fiscal third quarter revenue to $52.7 billion. However, if the historical pattern of growing by $4 to $5 billion continues in the fiscal second quarter, the company should beat analysts' estimates and report revenue around $48 billion.

We also know that Nvidia typically provides guidance that is $4 to $5 billion above the upper end of the range of the previous quarter's guidance. This trend has been evident since the fiscal third quarter of 2023; however, last quarter, the streak was broken, with a $2.0 billion increase over the prior quarter. If the company returns to its old ways, it should be able to guide revenue to between $50 billion and $51 billion. Which is where the problem starts, because the sell-side is looking for revenue for $52.7 billion, which means the company needs to guide the range to $52 to $53.5 billion for the mid-point range to meet anaylsts' estimates, that would be almost $7 billion more than last quarter's guidance at the upper end of the range. In fact, with consensus already near $53 billion, the company may need to guide investors to $53 to $55 billion to truly surprise them, or $8 billion above the prior quarter, which is about double its historical trends.

It isn't to say it can't be done; it is just noting that it is well outside the norm of what the company has been delivering for nearly two straight years.

Option Positions

Options positioning in Nvidia, as usual, is bullish going into the results. The biggest concentration of option gamma is at $180 for expiration on August 29, with the call wall at $185, and the put wall at $170. That means, unless Nvidia reports results that significantly exceed estimates and come in above historical norms, it will have a tough time surpassing $185, while being supported at around $170. These are likely to change somewhat as we approach the earnings report time.

Meanwhile, total delta values in the stock are positive and bullish. Unless the stock clears the call wall at $185, once the event risk passes and implied volatility drops, there will be calls to be unwound, which will put downward pressure on the shares. Basically, at this point, a $180 call with a premium paid of $5.28 won't be able to profit if the stock can't clear the call wall, if held until Friday.

This means that Nvidia will not only have to maintain its historical performance from a reported revenue standpoint but also provide guidance that exceeds its historical norms. It isn't to say that it can't be done, but as investors, we try to make educated decisions on outcomes that we think have some logic to them; otherwise, all we are doing is gambling.

This one clearly appears to be a gamble if you choose to place a bet on the stock rising significantly after it reports results. The company will need to report one of its best quarters ever and provide guidance that exceeds its past performance by almost double.

Nothing is impossible, but sometimes the best trade is the trade not made.
